caveats: a: The occupation source covers NAICS 532 broadly rather than NAICS 532282.
a: Surviving niche operators may use a different labor mix from the historical industry and from equipment-rental businesses.
rho: No industry survey measures five-year implementation of the defined AI-exposed opportunity.
rho: Business contraction can reduce both investment capacity and the payoff period for implementation.
e: The injected firm count is zero, so this eligibility share does not imply any presently available firms in the screened band.
e: The frozen firm-count estimate uses a margin bridge of 30 percent that may not fit distressed or mixed retail-rental operators.
s5: Distress events are not qualifying transfers unless an operating business changes control.
s5: The injected firm count is zero, making any conditional transfer probability practically secondary.
q: The FCC evidence concerns aggregate video spending and does not measure benefit pass-through by physical-rental operators.
q: Retention may be illusory if savings merely offset declining utilization rather than expand cash flow.
d5: The FCC series combines physical-media purchases and rentals rather than isolating NAICS 532282 output.
d5: Netflix's former mail-disc business differs operationally from stores and kiosks.
d5: The estimate extrapolates beyond observed history and is highly sensitive to survivor selection.
o: The estimate separates operator necessity from total demand decline imperfectly because streaming drives both.
o: Rare-title licensing changes could materially alter the residual physical basket.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Consumer rental fees, memberships, late or extension fees, and ancillary physical-media revenue tied to repeat use of an owned disc inventory.
lens.excluded_activities: Streaming subscriptions, transactional digital video, physical-media retail sales without a rental service, libraries, captive internal media operations, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US businesses whose recurring external-customer service is renting physical prerecorded video media through staffed locations, kiosks, or delivery channels and whose normalized EBITDA falls within the specified lower-middle-market band.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment shares across the broader rental and leasing subsector, not video rental or task-level wages. I treat counter, customer-service, billing, office, and portions of sales work as AI-exposed, while excluding most transport, material-moving, installation, and maintenance work. The broader subsector contains more equipment-intensive activity than video rental, which likely biases the inferred exposed share downward; task-to-wage mapping adds uncertainty in both directions.
caveats: The occupation source covers NAICS 532 broadly rather than NAICS 532282.
Surviving niche operators may use a different labor mix from the historical industry and from equipment-rental businesses.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from surviving video-rental operators, separating customer contact, merchandising, inventory handling, kiosk servicing, and management hours, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: The broader rental occupation mix shows substantial counter and rental clerks, sales, and administrative support that can be assisted through conversational service, catalog search, account handling, billing, and exception triage, but the physical inventory and kiosk or store work remains outside direct AI substitution.

d5.bridge: FCC reports that US retail spending on purchase and rental of physical video media fell from 11.8 billion dollars in 2012 to 1.5 billion dollars in 2023, while Netflix states that its disc business kept shrinking before its 2023 closure. I extrapolate a slower but still severe five-year decline for the remaining niche rental basket because survivors may be concentrated in collectors, hard-to-find titles, or underserved locations. The long historical interval and inclusion of physical sales can overstate rental decline, while survivor concentration can also make national data understate local collapse.
caveats: The FCC series combines physical-media purchases and rentals rather than isolating NAICS 532282 output.
Netflix's former mail-disc business differs operationally from stores and kiosks.
The estimate extrapolates beyond observed history and is highly sensitive to survivor selection.
d5.change_evidence: Annual same-location rental transactions, active memberships, and constant-price revenue from a panel of surviving stores and kiosks would materially change the five-year demand ratio.
d5.rationale: Physical-disc demand has already been displaced by streaming, and a major subscription-by-mail operator ended service after acknowledging continued shrinkage. Remaining demand is likely niche rather than a broad recurring consumer service.

caveats: The injected firm count is zero, so this eligibility share does not imply any presently available firms in the screened band.
The frozen firm-count estimate uses a margin bridge of 30 percent that may not fit distressed or mixed retail-rental operators.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level business-description review matched to revenue mix and normalized EBITDA, using Census microdata or a commercial establishment database plus operator confirmation, would establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: A surviving operator in the specified EBITDA band would ordinarily serve external repeat customers and therefore fit the lens, but some entities classified here may be sale-led collectibles businesses, captive media channels, or shells rather than recurring rental operations.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $509.0M / sectoral output (T30) $695.6M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.3000 (margins.json[532]: ESTIMATE: rental & leasing is asset-heavy with no clean Damodaran bucket (REITs 43.79% overstates operating rental); 30% chosen as a round mid-point before deriving results); revenue/employee $190,790 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 532282); class <5: 303 firms, avg revenue/firm $261,267 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $78,380 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 72 firms, avg revenue/firm $709,708 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $212,912 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 34 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,229,676 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $368,903 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,649,308 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $794,792 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 0

caveats: The estimate separates operator necessity from total demand decline imperfectly because streaming drives both.
Rare-title licensing changes could materially alter the residual physical basket.
o.change_evidence: Customer surveys at surviving operators that identify why each rental could not be replaced by owned media, streaming, library lending, or piracy would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Much of the current entertainment need can be self-served through streaming without a physical-rental operator. The residual basket includes titles unavailable digitally, collectors, customers with limited connectivity, and physical-format preferences, all of which still require inventory ownership, fulfillment, and accountability.

caveats: The FCC evidence concerns aggregate video spending and does not measure benefit pass-through by physical-rental operators.
Retention may be illusory if savings merely offset declining utilization rather than expand cash flow.
q.change_evidence: Store-level cohorts showing price changes, membership churn, contribution margin, and competitor response before and after operating-cost reductions would materially change retention assumptions.
q.rationale: Posted consumer prices and memberships do not mechanically pass labor savings through, so an operator can initially retain much of an implemented benefit. Retention is weakened by easy customer substitution, low switching costs, and the severe price and convenience benchmark set by streaming.

caveats: No industry survey measures five-year implementation of the defined AI-exposed opportunity.
Business contraction can reduce both investment capacity and the payoff period for implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor-system audits and owner interviews covering software stack, annual technology budget, transaction volumes, exception rates, and willingness to redesign work at a representative set of surviving operators would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Catalog discovery, routine customer support, membership messaging, basic purchasing, and reconciliation are technically straightforward to augment. Implementation is limited by very small operating teams, legacy point-of-sale or kiosk systems, sparse integration budgets, and the need to preserve reliable physical fulfillment.

caveats: Distress events are not qualifying transfers unless an operating business changes control.
The injected firm count is zero, making any conditional transfer probability practically secondary.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of closed and surviving operators linked to asset sales, bankruptcy dockets, business-broker listings, and control-transfer records would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Industry contraction may force ownership events, but closure, asset disposal, or liquidation is more plausible than a qualifying control transfer of a durable operating business. No transaction denominator for eligible video-rental firms was found.

principal_driver: Routine customer-service and administrative work remains amenable to AI assistance within surviving operations.
principal_weakness: Structural format substitution and an injected screened-firm count of zero dominate the five-year outlook.
